mixLCA 1.0.1

    • SLD theory and worked example

Synthetic Health-Screening Dataset with Distal Outcome

Source: R/data.R
health_screening.Rd

A simulated dataset of 800 subjects undergoing a health screening protocol. Four continuous biomarkers form a two-class latent structure; an antecedent age covariate shifts class membership; a binary screening outcome (yes / no) follows the latent class with a mild additional age effect.

Usage

health_screening

Format

A data frame with 800 rows and 6 columns.

marker_1

Numeric. Loosely log-normal continuous indicator (class-1 median around 80, class-2 around 140).

marker_2

Numeric. Loosely log-normal continuous indicator (class-1 median around 70, class-2 around 95).

marker_3

Numeric. Loosely log-normal continuous indicator (class-1 median around 22, class-2 around 33).

marker_4

Numeric. Loosely log-normal continuous indicator (class-1 median around 0.30, class-2 around 0.60).

age

Numeric. Age in years (truncated to 25-70).

outcome

Factor with levels no and yes: the binary distal outcome.

Source

Synthetic data, generated by data-raw/make-example-data.R.

Details

This dataset is designed to demonstrate the partitioned architecture supported by fit_lca: concomitant predictors (here age) enter the measurement model through multinomial logistic regression on class membership, while the distal outcome (outcome) is estimated separately via distal under Bolck-Croon-Hagenaars (BCH) inverse- classification-error weighting.

The data are synthetic, authored by the package author for didactic purposes, and are licensed under GPL (>= 3) along with the rest of the package. A reproducible generation script is provided in the source tree at data-raw/make-example-data.R.

We deliberately do not derive this dataset from any real screening cohort. Labelling participants by latent "metabolic class" can carry substantive implications that we leave to domain researchers; the synthetic construction here avoids importing those framings while preserving the joint structure (mixed continuous indicators, antecedent covariate, distal outcome) that the package is designed to model.

References

For methodological background on the partitioned LCA architecture used here, see Vermunt (2010), Latent class modeling with covariates: two improved three-step approaches, Political Analysis, 18(4), 450-469.

Examples

data(health_screening)
summary(health_screening)
#>     marker_1         marker_2         marker_3        marker_4     
#>  Min.   : 27.03   Min.   : 19.29   Min.   :10.70   Min.   :0.0848  
#>  1st Qu.: 65.78   1st Qu.: 57.75   1st Qu.:19.05   1st Qu.:0.2514  
#>  Median : 83.04   Median : 75.15   Median :23.07   Median :0.3224  
#>  Mean   : 92.72   Mean   : 78.94   Mean   :24.21   Mean   :0.3599  
#>  3rd Qu.:110.48   3rd Qu.: 93.70   3rd Qu.:27.78   3rd Qu.:0.4219  
#>  Max.   :445.89   Max.   :219.36   Max.   :71.77   Max.   :1.3325  
#>       age        outcome  
#>  Min.   :25.00   no :671  
#>  1st Qu.:36.40   yes:129  
#>  Median :44.35            
#>  Mean   :44.27            
#>  3rd Qu.:51.92            
#>  Max.   :70.00            
with(health_screening, table(outcome, age_bin = cut(age, c(20, 40, 55, 75))))
#>        age_bin
#> outcome (20,40] (40,55] (55,75]
#>     no      270     307      94
#>     yes      27      56      46
